title: Facial Chin Augmentation -- Preparation
content: Although these methods provide valuable insight into achieving an ideal chin position concerning the lips, face, and neck, only a few account for the crucial relationship between the nose and chin. This relationship is vital to acknowledge, as they directly influence each other's relative appearance in space. As a general rule, a more projected nose tends to make the chin appear less projected, and vice versa. An essential and ideal harmonious balance exists between the projection of the chin and the nose. Notably, all the mentioned analysis methods rely on standardized photography techniques, with four of them utilizing the Frankfort horizontal line—a well-established anatomical reference extending from the superior border of the external auditory canal to the inferior orbital rim. [5] Furthermore, radiographic evaluation, including panoramic radiographs and/or lateral and anterior-posterior (AP) radiographs, may be beneficial for addressing more complex deformities such as vertical height excess, orthognathic abnormalities, and transverse asymmetry, commonly observed in conditions such as hemifacial/craniofacial microsomia, oculoauricular vertebral (OAV) syndrome, or isolated anatomical components.

title: Orthodontics, Cephalometric Analysis -- Technique or Treatment -- Standardized Lateral Cephalometric Radiographs
content: Lateral skull radiographs provide a 2-dimensional representation of the head and neck and measure the sagittal and vertical dimensions of the skull. Sagittal measurements assess the position and inclination of the maxilla and mandible, while vertical measurements evaluate the height of the facial structures and the relationship between the jaw. [4] In contrast, posteroanterior radiographs, taken from the front of the head, measure transverse and vertical dimensions, offering insights into the width of the face and the relationship between the jaws in the transverse plane. [5] However, in clinical practice, cephalometric analysis primarily relies on lateral radiographs, as posteroanterior projections are more challenging to interpret.

title: Angiography -- Technique or Treatment
content: Imaging of the thoracic aorta begins by looking at the aortic root, the ascending aorta, the aortic arch, and the proximal descending aorta. The patient's head is tilted to the right and in the chin-up position for better access to the branches. An image of the arch at 40° LAO (left anterior oblique) is commonly acquired at the start of the procedure as it allows to describe the type of aortic arch which predicts the difficulty of the procedure, as type II and III arches make cannulation of the common carotid more difficult. title: Mandible Body Fracture -- Evaluation
content: Evaluation of the body fracture is via radiographs using plain radiography (panoramic, lateral-oblique, posteroanterior, occlusal, and periapical views) and CT scan. The lateral-oblique view helps to diagnose posterior body fractures. Mandibular occlusal view and Caldwell posteroanterior view demonstrate the presence of medial or lateral displacement of body fractures. Among all the radiographs, the most informative is the panoramic radiograph. The entire mandible is viewable in a single plane along with various advantages such as simplicity of technique, cost-effectiveness, and low radiation exposure compared with CT or cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT). However, it is challenging to take a panoramic radiograph in a severely traumatized patient as it usually requires the patient to be upright position. Also, as it is a 2-dimensional image, it becomes difficult to appreciate buccolingual bone displacement.

title: Epiglottitis -- Evaluation
content: As previously stated, pediatric epiglottitis should be diagnosed by clinical suspicion. No physical exam, laryngoscopy, laboratory test, or radiologic image is recommended. Securing the airway is the priority. The classic lateral neck radiograph should never be obtained without definitive airway control, and no child should be left unattended to acquire imaging. A lateral neck radiograph will show swelling of the epiglottis, also called the “thumb sign.” (See Image. Epiglottitis, Lateral X-Ray). The lateral x-ray is not necessary to diagnose, but it can be used to narrow the clinical differential diagnosis. Radiography should only be performed in the most stable, comfortable, and cooperative of patients. Approximately 20% of lateral neck radiographs will fail to accurately diagnose epiglottitis, underscoring the need for timely airway intervention and delay of laryngoscopy or other tests. The one exception that has been reported is obtaining computed tomography (CT) of the neck in early or unusual cases, but this should be done with caution because of the positioning of the patient and possible delay of airway control. [29]

title: Epiglottitis -- Evaluation
content: Adult epiglottitis evaluation may differ if urgent airway control is not warranted. Close monitoring and fiberoptic nasopharyngoscopy/laryngoscopy by otolaryngology are preferred, with an anesthesiologist available, quickly replacing the need for lateral neck soft-tissue radiography. If a lateral neck x-ray is performed, the criterion of 7-mm thickness (average standard epiglottic thickness is 3 to 5 mm) provides 100% sensitivity and specificity for acute adult epiglottitis. [34] A chest x-ray is helpful to rule out pneumonia; however, most otolaryngologists prefer neck CT if the patient is stable. Transcutaneous sublingual ultrasound scanning may become more valuable in the future. [35] Blood cultures may be positive in about 25% of H influenza cases. If the airway has been secured, epiglottic cultures are helpful, and some clinicians advocate aspiration of epiglottic abscesses with a spinal needle. [36]

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1725
content: Inspection (procedure) is a diagnostic category that incorporates several specific methods for visual and functional assessment of internal organs. Among its techniques are mediastinoscopy and thoracoscopy performed with ventilation of the lung, which allow direct visualization and sampling of mediastinal and pleural structures while maintaining adequate respiratory support. Another method included under inspection is the combination of paracentesis and endoscopic examination of the ileum, enabling fluid analysis together with direct endoscopic evaluation of the distal small intestine. In addition, inspection procedures serve as the method of choice for other bowel diagnostic procedures, encompassing a range of endoscopic and minimally invasive approaches used to assess gastrointestinal pathology. Together, these methods provide clinicians with essential information for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and optimal patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 516
content: CT angiography of thorax with contrast, Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material, followed by contrast material(s) and further sections, Computerized axial tomography (CT) scan, التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر, Other chest x‑ray and the category “ct scan chest” are interrelated components of thoracic imaging. The “ct scan chest” category represents chest computed tomography and includes specific techniques such as CT angiography of the thorax with contrast, which is used to visualize thoracic vascular structures, and the diagnostic thoracic CT protocol that begins without contrast and proceeds with contrast-enhanced sections. Computerized axial tomography (CT) scan is the broader imaging modality that has the child “التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر” (chest CT), positioning chest CT as a specialized subset of general CT scanning. In turn, chest CT serves as a parent category for both the specialized CT angiography and the diagnostic thoracic CT protocol, as well as for Other chest x‑ray, indicating that Other chest x‑ray is considered a related chest imaging study within the same hierarchical framework. This organized hierarchy clarifies how general CT technology narrows down to chest‑specific applications and how various chest imaging modalities are grouped for clinical decision‑making and patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1921
content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging study that can be directly linked to several more advanced computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is primarily mapped to both “CT of chest, abdomen and pelvis” and “Computerized tomography of chest,” indicating that these comprehensive and focused chest CT scans are the main follow‑up modalities when further anatomic detail is required beyond the plain radiograph. Additionally, routine chest x-ray is associated with other, less primary CT options, including “Computerized tomography of brain and chest” and “Computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium.” These alternative mappings reflect situations where clinicians may extend evaluation to include intracranial structures or require contrast enhancement for enhanced tissue characterization. Together, these relationships illustrate how a routine chest x-ray can guide the selection of targeted or extensive CT imaging, supporting diagnostic decision‑making and patient management.

title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2105
content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a foundational imaging reference that can be expanded into more detailed computed tomography (CT) examinations. It is the primary source from which a CT of the chest and abdomen and a CT of the chest are directly mapped, indicating a direct and principal relationship between the plain radiograph and these focused CT studies. Additionally, the routine chest x-ray is linked as an alternative or supplementary reference for broader CT examinations, including a computerized tomography of the brain and chest as well as a computerized tomography of the brain, chest, and abdomen. These mappings illustrate how a basic chest x-ray can inform and guide the selection of more comprehensive CT imaging of multiple thoracic and cranial regions, supporting nuanced diagnostic decision‑making and patient care.

The CT follow‑up procedure employs Computed tomography imaging – action as its method, and this Computed tomography imaging – action itself serves as a method of an Unlisted computed tomography procedure (eg, diagnostic, interventional). This hierarchical relationship links CT follow‑up examinations to broader radiology categories and specifies the CT imaging actions used in both diagnostic and interventional contexts.
